title: Infectious and parasitic diseases death rate
subtitle: The reported annual death rate from [infectious](#dod:communicable-diseases) and [parasitic diseases](#dod:parasitic-infection) per 100,000 people, based on the [underlying cause](#dod:underlying-cause-of-death) listed on death certificates.
caption: Between 1950 and 2023, the reported annual death rate from infectious and parasitic diseases per 100,000 people shows distinct trajectories across seven nations, with South Africa displaying a magnitude of fluctuation far exceeding the other countries. The vertical axis measures deaths per 100,000 people, ranging from 0 to 400, while the horizontal axis covers the 73-year period. South Africa’s data, beginning around 1996, rises from approximately 120 to a peak near 390 around 2006, before falling to roughly 130 by 2023. In contrast, the United States starts at the highest point among the other nations in 1950, near 80, and drops steadily to below 20 by 1980, remaining flat through 2023. France and Germany begin near 40 in 1950 and decline to levels below 10 by the 1970s. Brazil’s series starts in 1980 around 65 and decreases to approximately 30 by 2023. Russia and Australia show lower rates throughout their recorded periods, with Russia hovering between 20 and 30 from 1980 onward, and Australia remaining below 10 for the entire duration. The lines for the United States, France, Germany, Brazil, Russia, and Australia all cluster in the bottom section of the chart, staying below 70 for their entire visible ranges. The dominant visual pattern is the separation of South Africa’s curve, which reaches nearly four times the height of the next highest starting value, while the other six countries show a long-term decline or stable low rates.
